title: C String Functions
content:
### String Functions in C

The `<string.h>` header provides functions for string manipulation.

### Common String Functions

| Function | Description | Syntax |
|----------|-------------|--------|
| `strlen()` | Get string length | `size_t strlen(const char *s)` |
| `strcpy()` | Copy string | `char *strcpy(char *dest, const char *src)` |
| `strcat()` | Concatenate strings | `char *strcat(char *dest, const char *src)` |
| `strcmp()` | Compare strings | `int strcmp(const char *s1, const char *s2)` |
| `strncpy()` | Copy with limit | `char *strncpy(char *dest, const char *src, size_t n)` |
| `strncat()` | Concatenate with limit | `char *strncat(char *dest, const char *src, size_t n)` |
| `strncmp()` | Compare with limit | `int strncmp(const char *s1, const char *s2, size_t n)` |
| `strchr()` | Find character in string | `char *strchr(const char *s, int c)` |
| `strstr()` | Find substring | `char *strstr(const char *haystack, const char *needle)` |

### strcmp Return Values

| Return Value | Meaning |
|-------------|---------|
| `< 0` | s1 comes before s2 |
| `0` | s1 equals s2 |
| `> 0` | s1 comes after s2 |

### Safe Functions

Use `strncpy()` and `strncat()` instead of `strcpy()` and `strcat()` to prevent buffer overflows.

---

> **Key Point:** Always use bounded functions (`strncpy`, `strncat`) over unbounded ones to prevent buffer overflow vulnerabilities.
